items.title: Pourquoi l’algue « Ostreopsis » envahit-elle la côte basque chaque été ?
items.summary_excerpt: Que sait-on de la toxicité de cette algue ? Un projet de recherche est en cours pour répondre aux questions qui restent encore en suspens.

items.summary_excerpt: D’où vient l’or du Chéran, dans le massif des Bauges ? Élément de réponse : il ne s’est probablement pas formé sur place.

items.title: Tick bites are on the rise in the US and UK. Here’s how to protect yourself
items.summary_excerpt: Experts share tips to avoid the blood-sucking pests that can transmit Lyme disease and other illnesses on to humans I won’t go into gory detail about the tick I found latched behind my ear approximately 35 hours after a hike in early June. Suffice it to say, the parasite was well fed, and I was horrified. I am far from alone in making such a disgusting discovery. According to the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC), emergency room vi

items.title: Brazil's Amazon sees sharp decline in size of area burned in 2025
items.summary_excerpt: The amount of land burned in the world's largest rainforest was the smallest since MapBiomas records began in 1985.

items.title: ‘We couldn’t breathe’: DRC villagers revive protest against Perenco over pollution
items.summary_excerpt: “I had diarrhea and dizziness. Food no longer tasted the same. … I wasn’t the only one who noticed it — it was the same for everyone in the village; we couldn’t breathe,” Benoît Packera, secretary of the vigilance committee of Kitombe, told Mongabay. He was recounting what happened in April 2025, when toxic gases […]

items.title: How community-led health and livelihood programs helped protect rainforests during the pandemic
items.summary_excerpt: As COVID-19 disrupted rural economies, communities living near protected rainforests faced rising uncertainty about jobs, health and basic needs. A new study in The Lancet Planetary Health shows that when communities were supported by health and livelihood programs they designed with local nongovernmental organizations, both people and ecosystems were more resilient.

items.title: Geophysics brings clarity to the potential of natural hydrogen
items.summary_excerpt: Natural hydrogen is considered a potential low-carbon energy source. But how much is generated underground—and how quickly? An international research team led by the LIAG Institute for Applied Geophysics in Hannover has published the first process-based estimates for the Western Pyrenees and Northern California in Nature Communications. The calculated production rates are lower than earlier theoretical estimates. The study's new evaluation m

items.title: Parts of the UK move closer to drought
items.summary_excerpt: Eight areas across England are now in prolonged dry weather – the stage before drought.

items.title: Triassic inferno: Fires raged across Europe for millennia during past global warming
items.summary_excerpt: The end-Triassic mass extinction, 201 million years ago, is linked to volcanism that resulted from the break-up of Pangea and which pumped out vast amounts of CO2 into the atmosphere, causing extreme global warming of 5 to 10 degrees Celsius. As a result, tree-dominated vegetation collapsed and the highly disturbed landscapes were colonized by ferns that formed savannahs across large swaths of what is now Northwest Europe.

items.title: Extreme heat is eroding health, productivity and incomes of Dhaka’s informal workers
items.summary_excerpt: DHAKA — On June 28, as the afternoon sun beat down on Bangladesh capital Dhaka’s sprawling Tejgaon neighborhood, Samiha Begum gathered her two school-aged children and headed for the shade of Hatirjheel Lake. Their narrow one-room home in a nearby slum had become unbearable. She said, “The room starts heating up as soon as the […]

items.title: Slums are bearing the brunt of the climate crisis—and devising solutions
items.summary_excerpt: As temperatures rise, it is the poor who suffer most. The coping strategies of those living in informal settlements may hold lessons for cities of the future.

items.title: Report reveals Cambodia’s appetite for tigers, rhinos and other endangered wildlife
items.summary_excerpt: Tiger bone wines in exquisite bottles. Tiger cubs sold alive as pets or served as a culinary delicacy alongside pangolin and cobra meat at exclusive restaurants. Rooms filled with rhino horns and elephant ivory, stacks of freshly skinned tiger pelts and bags of pangolin scales. Jewelry made from protected corals and ivory. This is just […]

items.title: Ancient mystery on K’gari as world’s largest sand island lakes dried up during rainy era
items.summary_excerpt: Ancient sediment reveals that some of K’gari’s deepest lakes vanished around 7,500 years ago, despite unusually heavy rainfall. The surprising discovery suggests changing winds and future climate shifts could threaten the sacred lakes known as the Eyes of K’gari.

items.title: China’s Chang’e-6 reveals why solar wind hits the Moon’s two sides differently
items.summary_excerpt: China’s Chang’e-6 samples have uncovered a surprising difference between the Moon’s two hemispheres. Solar wind particles penetrated deeper into the far-side soil because Earth’s magnetosphere slows the particles that reach the near side. Noble gases locked inside the lunar regolith preserved evidence of this uneven bombardment. These gases may even help scientists reconstruct the ancient history of Earth’s magnetic shield.

items.title: We received no alert, say leaders of First Nations communities razed by Canadian wildfires
items.summary_excerpt: Chiefs in Ontario say they were left to self-evacuate before homes burned to ground, after being told there was no imminent threat Fast-moving wildfires have burned two First Nations communities in Canada to the ground, as members said they were left to self-evacuate without government help or emergency alerts. Thirteen communities in Ontario province have been ordered to evacuate because of wildfires, most of them First Nations. The Ontario prem

items.title: Pangolin DNA study links local wild meat markets to global trafficking networks
items.summary_excerpt: Pangolin scales and other body parts trafficked internationally appear to come from the same areas that supply the animals for local wild meat markets, a new study has found. It created a DNA database and location-prediction model for three threatened species: the white-bellied (Phataginus tricuspis), Sunda (Manis javanica) and Chinese (Manis pentadactyla) pangolins. Pangolins are […]

items.title: UK's bumblebees are struggling in heatwaves
items.summary_excerpt: UK bumblebees could suffer sharp falls in their numbers as heatwaves become more frequent.

items.title: What happened to the vessels Panama busted in 2025 for fishing in its flagship MPA?
items.summary_excerpt: On Jan. 22, 2025, Panama’s government announced it had caught 16 vessels fishing illegally inside the country’s flagship marine protected area, declaring it the largest such enforcement operation in the country’s history. Publicizing the case helped Panama show its commitment to ensure 30% of its waters are “effectively conserved and managed” by 2030, in compliance […]

items.title: Rescuers in Afghanistan search for missing and dead after flash floods destroy homes
items.summary_excerpt: National disaster agency confirms ‘severe financial and human losses’ in eastern province of Nuristan Rescue crews in Afghanistan are searching for survivors and bodies among the rubble of houses destroyed by devastating flash floods that authorities said had killed nearly two dozen people and left more than 100 people missing. The Afghanistan national disaster management authority said on Tuesday that 23 people had died, 80 had been injured and

items.title: As Sri Lanka braces for El Niño, national parks are a concern amidst encroachment
items.summary_excerpt: COLOMBO — Around 14% of Sri Lanka’s total land area is dedicated to wildlife protected areas, which includes more than 20 national parks. However, due to natural and anthropogenic factors, protected species such as leopards (Panthera pardus kotiya) and elephants (Elephas maximus maximus) living in these protected areas face continuous threats as their habitats continue […]

items.title: Activists allege Cambodia has resumed sea sand exports to Singapore
items.summary_excerpt: BANGKOK — Environmental activism group Mother Nature Cambodia on July 14 posted a new video alleging that Cambodia has quietly resumed sand exports to Singapore after the trade was banned in 2017. The prohibition followed a scandal involving environmental degradation along Cambodian rivers linked to shipments of sand sold to Singapore for land reclamation projects. […]

items.title: Fires in Brazil's Amazon dropped to historic low in 2025
items.summary_excerpt: Forest fires in the Brazilian Amazon fell to a historic low in 2025 after peaking the previous year, according to a report published Tuesday.

items.title: Zero roadkill as Amazon canopy bridges secure 15,000 crossings
items.summary_excerpt: For some of the world's rarest monkeys, the path to survival now runs through a simple network of ropes.

items.title: Piqûres de moustiques : toutes les réponses à vos questions
items.summary_excerpt: « Sus aux moustiques ! » (1/4). Pourquoi les piqûres de moustiques provoquent-elles des démangeaisons ? Certaines personnes sont-elles davantage ciblées que d’autres ? Du mécanisme de la réaction cutanée à la transmission des virus, ce qu’il faut savoir sur ces insectes qui rythment nos étés.

items.title: Eight lifesaving ways to improve wildfire risk management
items.summary_excerpt: As the flames advanced toward the village of Bédar in Spain's Almería province, the mayor gave a desperate order to ring the church bells to warn residents. It was not enough. The July 9 Los Gallardos fire, which claimed 13 lives and burned 7,000 hectares (17,300 acres), was Spain's third-deadliest on record.

items.title: Renato Moraes de Jesus was a pioneer of Atlantic Forest restoration in Brazil
items.summary_excerpt: In 1977 a young forest engineer arrived at a neglected tract of Atlantic Forest in northern Espírito Santo. A mining company had acquired the property as a source of hardwood for railway sleepers. Two hundred hectares had already been cut. Some specialists proposed harvesting more of the forest or replacing it with eucalyptus. The new […]

items.title: Incendies au Canada : déjà 3 millions d’hectares ravagés par les flammes, une saison des feux de forêt « intense »
items.summary_excerpt: Si la puissance des incendies n’atteint pas celle de la saison 2023, qui avait été dévastatrice, de nouveaux records pourraient tout de même être battus cette année, notamment à cause de faibles précipitations dans une partie du pays.

items.title: Bumblebees are struggling in UK heatwaves - but you can help them
items.summary_excerpt: The furry bodies of UK bumblebees are specially adapted for cold weather - making heatwaves a struggle.

items.title: Malaysia clamps down on toxic trade with total e-waste import ban
items.summary_excerpt: KLANG, Malaysia — On the morning of July 7, 2026, a shipping container declared to contain tin ore caught fire shortly after offloading at Port Klang, Malaysia. After firefighters doused the flames, they pried open the steel doors and found no industrial metals — only a cargo packed full of scorched electrical and electronic waste, […]

items.title: Canada faces one of its most intense fire seasons
items.summary_excerpt: With nearly 1,000 active fires and an area as large as Belgium already burned, Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney says his country is facing one of the "most intense" wildfire seasons in its history.

items.title: Surfing webcams help demystify Oregon's coastal fog
items.summary_excerpt: A summer beach day in Oregon often involves a cool, gray blanket of fog that not only shapes the experience of beachgoers but also affects the coastal and inland environment. Now, commercially available surfing webcams are helping scientists measure Oregon's coastal fog, according to a study from the University of California, Davis, in collaboration with the USDA Forest Service.

items.title: Making Weather and Climate Information Reach the Communities that Need It
items.summary_excerpt: Many at-risk communities remain unreached by forecasts due to barriers in language, access, and trust in information systems. A new study looks at how to overcome these.

items.title: Slowdown in Arctic ice melt ends after largest winter decline on record
items.summary_excerpt: Scientists recorded slower reduction over past 20 years, but study finds this was ‘temporary rather than sustained’ A previously recorded slowdown in Arctic sea ice decline has ended, according to a new study, with the largest year-to-year wintertime reduction ever recorded. In a study published last summer, researchers found that melting sea ice in the Arctic had slowed down over the past 20 years, with no statistically significant decline in it

items.title: Arctic winter sea ice returns to significant decline, ending the temporary slowdown
items.summary_excerpt: New analysis shows that a "pause" in Arctic winter sea ice decline seen in the early 2020s has ended following record lows in 2025 and early 2026. The study, led by the University of Southampton in collaboration with the National Oceanography Center (NOC), adds the most recent satellite observations to sea ice coverage trends during the winter growth and peak phases of the Arctic sea ice cycle.

items.title: WHO evacuated health workers from Ebola treatment center due to armed clashes
items.summary_excerpt: On July 15, gunfire erupted between armed groups near an Ebola Treatment Center (ETC) in Nyankunde (or Nyakunde), located 45 kilometers (28 miles) southwest of Bunia in the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC). Following the fighting, medical personnel from the World Health Organization (WHO), Africa CDC and the NGO Samaritan’s Purse evacuated from Nyankunde, […]

items.title: Tiny trackers reveal where migratory shorebirds need protection across Africa
items.summary_excerpt: They have names like Victoria, Makgadikgadi, Wembere and Dwarskersbos — but unlike their namesakes, they’re not landmarks in Africa. Instead, they’re small birds flying halfway across the world, showing conservationists the places that should be protected to sustain them on their journeys. Earlier this year, an international team of scientists and conservationists trapped 20 curlew […]

items.title: Canadian wildfires rage as Trump warns Carney again over 'poisoning' US air
items.summary_excerpt: The US president raised the issue with the the Canadian prime minister while the pair attended the World Cup Final in New Jersey.

items.title: Microplastic tire pollution found in Brisbane balcony dust
items.summary_excerpt: Tire particles and potentially toxic tire chemical additives have been found in dust collected from Brisbane residential balconies. University of Queensland researchers examined the balcony dust from homes across inner-city, suburban and near-road settings to see how traffic-related pollution traveled from streets to residential environments.

items.title: L’été de tous les records ne surprend pas les climatologues : « On est entrés dans une zone dangereuse »
items.summary_excerpt: Records de chaleur, sécheresse historique, incendies de grande ampleur… Derrière une période estivale qui paraît dépasser toutes les limites, les climatologues voient surtout la confirmation des effets attendus du réchauffement. Seul l’enchaînement des canicules les intrigue.

items.title: Clean cooking expands at record pace in Africa, but lags behind population growth
items.summary_excerpt: Sub-Saharan Africa is on track to record its fastest-ever annual expansion in access to clean cooking in 2026, according to a new report by the International Energy Agency. But population growth continues to outpace those gains, leaving more than 1 billion people without access to clean cooking. The region is home to the world’s largest […]

items.title: Food labeling combined with higher prices for carbon-intensive foods could help reduce UK greenhouse gases by 5%
items.summary_excerpt: Economists say labeling supermarket food to show its environmental impact, combined with taxing the most carbon-intensive items, could slash UK greenhouse gas emissions by almost 5% while generating net gains for society.

items.summary_excerpt: Air polluters try to ensure toxic emissions happen far from monitors – but the people who live nearby still feel it Kimbrelle Kyereh lives just a few hundred feet from the massive chemical storage tanks in St Rose, in south-eastern Louisiana . items.title: Swiss researchers hoard vast trove of NASA climate data
items.summary_excerpt: Researchers have copied massive quantities of publicly available climate and environmental data from NASA onto a Swiss supercomputer to train artificial intelligence models—and for safekeeping amid U.S. funding cuts.

items.title: Earth’s waters are quietly running out of oxygen, scientists warn
items.summary_excerpt: Oxygen is disappearing from oceans, lakes, rivers, and coastal waters at an alarming rate, threatening aquatic life and weakening the natural processes that help regulate Earth’s climate. Scientists warn that this widespread deoxygenation is closely connected to other major planetary threats, including warming, pollution, biodiversity loss, and ocean acidification.

items.title: Africa's wildfire season is actually getting shorter, scientists think they know why
items.summary_excerpt: Africa is home to around 70% of the world's burned area—the total area of land scorched by wildfires each year—making it the planet's biggest wildfire hotspot. Yet despite this, satellite observations have revealed an unexpected trend over the past two decades: The amount of land burned across the continent has been steadily falling.

items.title: Réchauffement climatique : « Changer de méthode, c’est d’emblée agir en très grand, et c’est possible »
items.summary_excerpt: Un collectif de chercheurs et de spécialistes de la forêt estime, dans une tribune au « Monde », que l’atténuation du changement climatique n’est pas une gageure. Le rôle que pourraient jouer les forêts dans une telle stratégie en est un bon exemple.

items.title: How to keep the air inside your home clean during wildfires
items.summary_excerpt: The eerie glow of an orange sun through thick wildfire smoke might be unnerving, but the biggest risk from the haze is something much harder to see.

items.title: Greece braces for year's first heat wave
items.summary_excerpt: Greece, which was spared the record heat that ravaged most of Europe in June, is finally bracing for its first heat wave of the year.

items.title: Going with the flow: Renewable energy battery breakthrough
items.summary_excerpt: A 3D-printed battery designed at Queen's University Belfast could speed up energy storage research.

items.title: Wildfire haze hangs over eastern US—and World Cup final
items.summary_excerpt: Heavy, acrid wildfire smoke blanketed much of the eastern United States on Saturday, marring preparations for the weekend's highly anticipated World Cup final to be held in an open stadium in New Jersey.

items.summary_excerpt: Despite the backing of councils in a region in dire need of support, the federal government has rejected a $42m grant request to complete the trail’s 54km missing link Get our breaking news email , free app or daily news podcast There are glow-worms in the old train tunnel if you look hard enough into the deepest, darkest black, Paul Hixon says. The visitor from Brisbane is cycling the Murwillumbah to Crabbes Creek section of the northern rivers

items.title: Après les feux, le lent retour de la forêt : « On a le sentiment qu’il n’y a plus du tout de vie, mais en réalité il y en a encore »
items.summary_excerpt: Difficiles à quantifier, les conséquences écologiques des incendies varient en fonction de nombreux critères. L’intensité et la fréquence des feux peuvent être particulièrement délétères pour les écosystèmes.

items.title: Les canicules à répétition font exploser l’écoanxiété et la colère des jeunes : « J’ai l’impression qu’on est entrés dans une dystopie »
items.summary_excerpt: Dans le sillage de la troisième vague de chaleur qui s’est abattue sur la France depuis mai, la frustration et l’angoisse s’imposent chez une partie des jeunes générations, confrontées à « l’été le plus frais du reste de leur vie ».

items.title: Trump threatens new Canada tariffs over fires sending 'filthy' air into US cities
items.summary_excerpt: Canadian leader Mark Carney says both the US and Canada have an equal responsibility to fight climate change, which experts say are worsening wildfire conditions.

items.title: Toronto, New York and Washington top list of most polluted cities as wildfires burn
items.summary_excerpt: Canadian officials say that fire activity in Ontario is beginning to ease, with air quality expected to improve in US cities too.

items.title: They were drilling off Oregon. What they found could shake the entire West Coast
items.summary_excerpt: Scientists have found evidence that a major Cascadia earthquake could trigger a second quake on the San Andreas Fault. The discovery came from unusual sediment layers formed by underwater landslides recorded over thousands of years. These patterns suggest both fault systems may have ruptured close together in the past. The findings raise new concerns about a potential coast-wide earthquake sequence.

items.title: A shattered asteroid may have bombarded Earth 800 million years ago
items.summary_excerpt: A catastrophic asteroid breakup may have triggered a huge wave of impacts across the inner solar system about 800 million years ago. The debris was launched from near a gravitational gateway controlled by Jupiter, sending fragments toward Earth, the Moon, and Mars. The bombardment may explain ancient lunar craters and could have contributed to major climate and biological changes on Earth.

items.title: Earth’s biggest volcanic event transformed an entire oceanic plate
items.summary_excerpt: Seismic waves have revealed that the oceanic plate beneath the Ontong Java Plateau was dramatically transformed by the colossal volcanic activity that created it more than 100 million years ago. Researchers found a complex structure of horizontal layers cut through by vast swarms of vertical magma channels, along with unusually slow seismic waves suggesting that deep-rising magma chemically altered the plate itself.

items.title: Dengue, Zika, chikungunya : les moustiques invasifs coûtent des milliards, la prévention reste à la traîne
items.summary_excerpt: Les maladies transmises par les moustiques Aedes ont engendré au moins 94,7 milliards dollars de pertes dans le monde. Les estimations hautes suggèrent elles un coût total de plus de 300 milliards.

items.title: Après trois canicules, des Français sidérés et inquiets : « L’année prochaine ça va être pire, comment on va faire ? »
items.summary_excerpt: De la Bretagne aux Bouches-du-Rhône, de Paris à Périgueux, les Français ont découvert leur vulnérabilité aux températures extrêmes qui ont touché durablement leur pays ces dernières semaines.

items.title: Alexis Jenni, écrivain : « Les branches noircies des arbres dressées vers le ciel deviennent des membres implorants »
items.summary_excerpt: Face aux incendies qui ravagent les forêts, le romancier et essayiste rappelle que ce n’est pas qu’un écosystème qui s’effondre sous nos yeux, mais « notre milieu même ». Pendant ce temps, déplore-t-il, dans une tribune au « Monde », les responsables politiques se montrent plus empressés à défaire les avancées environnementales qu’à protéger véritablement les bois.

Crustose coralline algae buffer shallow reef environments from dissolution

items.title: A Bordeaux, entre deux vagues de chaleur, les équipes de l’hôpital Pellegrin sous forte pression
items.summary_excerpt: « Coup de chaud » et détresse respiratoire… Les différents services du CHU ont dû serrer les rangs pour prendre en charge les patients souffrant des effets de la canicule. Grâce, notamment, au protocole de « refroidissement thérapeutique », la situation semble maîtrisée.

items.title: Big Trouble from Little Wetlands
items.summary_excerpt: New research suggests that small wetlands, previously unaccounted for in the global methane budget, are responsible for about 25% of global methane emissions.

items.title: Six livres sur les oiseaux à feuilleter sur la plage ou en forêt
items.summary_excerpt: Chaleur humaine vous propose six ouvrages pour mieux connaître les oiseaux les plus proches de nous, et pour découvrir notre série d’épisodes « Envole-Moi »

items.title: As the UK and Europe battle deadly wildfires, what lessons can Australia offer?
items.summary_excerpt: Knowledge learned over more than a century in Australia is being tested by worsening fires. It’s a familiar narrative around the world The violent hot red flames of deadly wildfires across the UK and Europe and scenes of panicked communities fleeing homes could not, at least geographically, be further away for Jan Harris. But sitting in her new home at Reedy Swamp in rural New South Wales in Australia, the 67-year-old has found herself in tears.

items.title: Win–wins for wind and whales
Tropical forest restoration can offset water flux losses from deforestation but not everywhere
Balancing marine species conservation with cost-effective renewable energy development
Intercity connectivity enhances urban mobility resilience to extreme rainfall
The hydrological asymmetric signature of El Niño–Southern Oscillation on global water storage
We’re Getting Better at Knowing When Climate Change is to Blame, National Academies Report Says
items.summary_excerpt: Scientists have gotten much better at parsing how severe events are linked to climate change, a long-awaited report from the National Academies of Sciences, Engineering and Medicine has found. The report was developed by 14 experts (including climatologists, meteorologists, and atmospheric scientists) and updates a 2016 report on the same subject. That report found that climate change was partly responsible for worsening heat waves, cold events,

items.title: The “Eternity Glaciers” Are Almost Gone
items.summary_excerpt: The equatorial glaciers atop Puncak Jaya, Oceania’s tallest peak, have shrunk by about 95% since 2002, according to a new photogrammetry mapping effort.

items.title: Earth has a natural thermostat and scientists finally know how it works
items.summary_excerpt: Scientists have identified a hidden feedback loop that may explain how Earth has regulated its climate for tens of millions of years. As sea levels rose and fell, they changed how much phosphate reached the open ocean, affecting marine life and the amount of carbon buried beneath the seafloor. That burial removed carbon dioxide from the atmosphere, helping cool the planet.

items.title: Quelle application météo utilisez-vous ? Racontez-nous
items.summary_excerpt: De nombreux sites et applications, gratuits comme payants, font désormais concurrence à Météo-France. Vous utilisez un de ces services ? Dites-nous pourquoi.

items.title: El Niño pourrait faire bondir l’usage de la climatisation et poser « des défis immédiats » aux systèmes électriques, selon l’Agence internationale de l’énergie
items.summary_excerpt: L’organisation appelle à améliorer l’efficacité des climatiseurs et à mettre en place des réponses politiques globales pour répondre aux besoins croissants de refroidissement.

items.title: Percevoir les conséquences immédiates du changement climatique suffit-il pour changer les comportements ?
items.summary_excerpt: Cet été, la France subit de plein fouet les conséquences directes du changement climatique. Cela peut-il être utile pour modifier les comportements ? La réponse est moins évidente qu’il n’y paraît.

items.title: Les refuges climatiques seront utiles s’ils n’oublient pas de faire de la chaleur un objet politique
items.summary_excerpt: Notamment à condition de les concevoir et de les déployer avec des critères d’accessibilité, d’hospitalité et de justice socioécologique. En bref, d’en faire des objets politiques.

items.title: La France, éreintée par les canicules, adopte une feuille de route pour être neutre en carbone en 2050, qui devra démontrer sa crédibilité
items.summary_excerpt: Mercredi, le ministère de la transition écologique a présenté la troisième stratégie nationale bas carbone, le document qui fixe le cap de la réduction des émissions de gaz à effet de serre jusqu’en 2050. Les objectifs sont très ambitieux au regard des politiques menées jusqu’à présent.

items.title: These “Clumped” Molecules Could Offer Clues About Earth’s Climate
items.summary_excerpt: Researchers investigated concentrations of clumped methane isotopes to learn more about how levels of the potent greenhouse gas have changed over the industrial era.

items.title: UK withdraws millions in funding from world’s second-largest rainforest in Congo
items.summary_excerpt: The UK has abandoned projects worth tens of millions of pounds that were meant to... The post UK withdraws millions in funding from world’s second-largest rainforest in Congo appeared first on Carbon Brief .
